coredata.dc:title: Bio-quantum dots for electrochemical sensing of cardiac biomarkers of acute myocardial infarction
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry

coredata.dc:creator: Arumugasamy, Shiva Kumar
Chellasamy, Gayathri
Yun, Kyusik
Hyun, Jinho
coredata.dc:description: Cellulose nanofibers (CNFs) are utilized as a biotemplate for facile fabrication of quantum dots via a one-step method. The structural integrity of quantum dots with radical active sites and probable conjugation sites with reactive groups was comprehensively studied using optical absorbance, NMR, PL, FTIR, Raman, XPS, and UPS analyses. Acute myocardial infarction (AMI) is detected by electrochemical biosensing of a cardiac biomarker using a troponin protein family sensing molecule. Cardiac troponin I (cTnI) conjugated with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was used as a biomimetic receptor for immunosensing of cTnI with a detection limit of 29 pM. The cTnI-based biosensor enables selective sensing in the presence of various interferents. Calibration and validation of the cTnI sensor platform were demonstrated in a PBS medium with successful implementation of a point-of-care indium tin oxide (ITO) electrode for measurement. For the proof-of-concept, a GQD-modified flexible ITO electrode was administered for electrochemical sensing of cTnI at a low sample concentration and showed promising results for cardiovascular diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cellulose nanofibers
Graphene quantum dots
Cardiac troponin I
Immunosensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This paper was supported by Korea Evaluation Institute of Industrial Technology (KEIT) grant funded by the Korea Government (MOTIE) (20023781). This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education ( 2021R1A6A03038996 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) optical absorbance of as-synthesized gqds. b) pl spectrum for developed gqds. c) pl spectrum for assessing excitation-dependent emission. d) X-ray diffraction analysis for GQDs. e) FTIR spectra of synthesized GQDs, and f) Raman spectral analysis of synthesized GQDs.

A)-d) tem images of gqds at different magnifications. e), f) hrtem images of gqds. g) fft images obtained for gqds. h), i), j) AFM images, height profile, and 3D morphology of synthesized GQDs. k) Size measurement obtained by evaluating the TEM image using Image J and l) zeta sizer and surface charge of the synthesized GQDs.
A), b)1H and 13C NMR spectral survey; c)-h) magnified regime of 1H NMR; and i)-k) magnified region of 13C NMR study.

A) xps full survey spectrum for gqds and gqds/c + Ab-Ag. b) C1s deconvolution spectrum. c) N 1 s deconvoluted spectrum. d) O 1 s deconvolution spectrum. e) UPS full survey spectrum and f) magnified full survey spectrum.
Schematic illustration of cTnI immunosensor fabrication by flexible ITO electrode. (Created with BioRender.com).

A), b) cv and eis analyses of the fabricated immunosensor in 2.5 mM Fe(CN)6 3-/4- medium. c) Scan rate-dependent CV study (20 – 200 mV/s). d) Corresponding scan rate vs. current. e) Linear fit for square root of scan rate and change in potential and f) linear fit for logarithmic scale of scan rate vs. change in current.

A) lsv analysis for gqds/c + Ab electrodes vs. concentration of cTnI protein in PBS. b) Relative linear fit obtained from LSV study. c) DPV analysis for GQDs/C + Ab electrodes vs. concentration of cTnI protein in PBS. d) Linear plot calculated for an immunosensor against cTnI proteins. e) Chronoamperometric analysis of GQDs/C + Ab electrodes vs. concentration of cTnI protein in PBS and f) linear plot of immunosensor against cTnI proteins.
A) Nyquist plot, b) Bode plot for GQDs/C + Ab electrode vs. concentration of cTnI protein in Fe(CN)6 3-/4- medium. c) Linear fit calculated for the immunosensor against resistance obtained.
Visualization of the synthesis methodology for graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from carboxy methylated-cellulose nanofibers (CM-CNFs). (Created with BioRender.com).

head.ce:title: Bio-quantum dots for electrochemical sensing of cardiac biomarkers of acute myocardial infarction

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Agriculture, Forestry, and Bioresources, Seoul National University, Seoul 08826, Republic of Korea (J. Hyun), Department of Bionanotechnology, Gachon University, Seongnam-si, 13120, Republic of Korea (K. Yun).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphical abstract for the fabrication of cTnI immunosensor.

Cellulose nanofibers (CNFs) are utilized as a biotemplate for facile fabrication of quantum dots via a one-step method. The structural integrity of quantum dots with radical active sites and probable conjugation sites with reactive groups was comprehensively studied using optical absorbance, NMR, PL, FTIR, Raman, XPS, and UPS analyses. Acute myocardial infarction (AMI) is detected by electrochemical biosensing of a cardiac biomarker using a troponin protein family sensing molecule. Cardiac troponin I (cTnI) conjugated with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was used as a biomimetic receptor for immunosensing of cTnI with a detection limit of 29 pM. The cTnI-based biosensor enables selective sensing in the presence of various interferents. Calibration and validation of the cTnI sensor platform were demonstrated in a PBS medium with successful implementation of a point-of-care indium tin oxide (ITO) electrode for measurement. For the proof-of-concept, a GQD-modified flexible ITO electrode was administered for electrochemical sensing of cTnI at a low sample concentration and showed promising results for cardiovascular diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: A report from the WHO estimated that 17.9 million people globally are affected by cardiovascular diseases, 38% of which result in death due to heart attack or stroke [1]. Acute myocardial infarction (AMI) is a heart condition caused by blockage of blood circulation from the heart. Inaccurate diagnosis of AMI can lead to morbidity and mortality, demonstrating the importance of immediate treatment. The low identification rate of early-stage AMI is due to either symptom confusion between AMI and stomach gastritis or inaccurate electrocardiogram interpretation due to detection techniques with low sensitivity [2,3]. To overcome these drawbacks, biomarker proteins (enzymes) seem to be a potential candidate for easy diagnosis, earlier risk assessment, and evaluation of therapy effectiveness. Biomarkers are released by damaged or stressed organs and circulate in the bloodstream, with the potential for rapid detecting and increasing the likelihood of accurate diagnosis. Cardiac biomarker detection could demonstrate the status of heart conditions due to necrosis, ischemia, stress, or inflammation depending on the enzymes released [4–6]. During cardiac necrosis, troponin, creatine kinase, myoglobin, ischemic albumin, fatty-acid binding protein, stress natriuretic peptides, inflammation C-reactive protein, and homocysteine are released and can be sensed in the bloodstream at various intervals [7–9]. Cardiac Troponin possess to be an ideally suitable candidate in complex clinical situations for detecting myocardial infarction. The observation of elevations of creatine-kinase MB isoform in patients with acute or chronic muscle disease or renal failure was present whilst cTnI evidence only during cardiac injury is present. In human myocardium, cTnI proteolysis is more extensive and complex because of the heterogeneity of the diseases. The sensitivity of cTnI is about > 0.9 μg/L while for CKMB it is about > 8 μg/L which provides the necessity of sensing cardiac Troponin [10,11]. Generally, troponins remain elevated in the bloodstream for around 10 days and show better sensitivity than other cardiac biomarkers.

Cellulose, a linear natural polysaccharide consisting of repetitive 1,4-anhydro-D-glucopyranose units linked by glycosidic ligands, is an inexhaustible natural bio-source that forms a crystalline structure that can aggregate to form fibers. Cellulose is a colorless, odorless, non-toxic polymer possessing properties such as hydrophilicity, thermostability, high adsorption, tunable optics, and biocompatibility [12–14]. Cellulosic nanomaterials represent 50% of natural biomass, are typically 5 – 20 nm in diameter and 2––5 μm in length, form a homogeneous suspension, and are colloidally stable. Carboxymethylated cellulose nanofibers (CM-CNFs) are surface modified by ionic CNFs of pulp produced by mechanical grinding, forming a fibrous network due to the high aspect ratio. CM-CNFs possess weak interfibrillar hydrogen bonds that are strengthened by higher valence cations and stronger ionic interactions [15–17]. CM-CNFs can be chemically oxidized, and the nanofibers are separated to form quantum dots (QDs). Generally, QDs are formed due to quantum confinement and edge effects on the carbon sources with addition of amine and acid precursors, which aid in cleaving the bulk carbon materials [18,19]. QDs prepared from polymeric materials often have the drawbacks of complicative structures, polymeric chains, and toxicity, while CNF-based QDs do not have these deficiencies. QDs are based elementally and structurally on excess hydroxyl and ethyl moieties. Since cellulose has stable chemical properties, it is complicated to synthesize QDs under normal pressure and temperature. GQDs exhibit molecule-like properties, possessing tunable band gap and luminescence on excitation. Bandgap tunability depends on the size and surface chemistry of GQDs. Most of the GQDs observe hold up-conversion luminescence due to the anti-stokes transition where electrons in π-orbital are excited and σ-orbital electrons are in relaxed state [20]. Additionally, GQDs are easily soluble in various solvents equipped with functional moieties at their edge lattices. GQDs possess distinct and intriguing properties such as narrower and complex bandgap with abundant functional moieties comparable to the CDs. Furthermore, CDs are majorly amorphous in nature whilst GQDs are highly crystalline in nature [21]. To overcome these difficulties, we used a hydrothermal method of thermal decomposition and carbonization to obtain uniform QDs [22].

Considering the advantages of flexible indium tin oxide (ITO) electrodes and novel redox active QD catalysts derived from CM-CNFs, we developed biosensors for cardiac biomarkers to detect cardiac troponin I (cTnI) antigen. QDs have a negative surface on the edge and basal planar functional moieties that are useful for electrostatic interactions. Surface integration of bio-interfacial layers with carbamide and succinimide layers can be used to retain the bioactivity of immobilized antibodies. The biomarker protein (cTnI) has been selected to test the immune-sensing activity of the proposed sensor to enable early diagnosis of myocardial infarction. The mechanism behind the sensor involves formation of electro-adsorbing features (providing bio-affinity) for immunocomplexation reactions at the surface of the interfacial electrodes. Optimization of the sensor platform at applied potentials for chronoamperometric studies was explored using fundamental electrochemical techniques. Fluorescence quenching-mediated analysis of cTnI protein was also investigated. This approach provides a single nanoprobe screen that can be used in portable optical and electrochemical diagnostics. [23].

Never-dried bleached eucalyptus kraft pulp (79.4% ± 0.6% cellulose, 18.8% ± 0.2% hemicellulose and very lignin and byproducts) was obtained from Moorim P&P (Ulsan, Korea) [16]. Methanol (99.5%), ethanol (anhydrous 99.9%), and isopropanol (99.5%) were procured from Samchun, Seoul, Korea. Chloroacetic acid (≥99.0%), NaCl (≥99.0%), MgCl2 (anhydrous ≥ 98.0%), and CaCl2 (anhydrous ≥ 97.0%) were procured from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Monoclonal mouse anti-cardiac troponin I (cTnI) catalogue 4 T21/4T21cc and human cardiac troponin I (cTnI) catalogue 8 T53 were purchased from HyTest Ltd. (Turku, Finland). All chemicals were utilized without further purification.

Never-dried bleached eucalyptus kraft pulp was beaten using a laboratory valley beater for about 30 mins. The beaten pulp (dry weight, 70 g) was solvent-exchanged with ethanol, immersed in an isopropanol/methanol (3.2 L:0.8 L) solution with sodium hydroxide (11.2 g), and heated at 65 °C for 60 min under constant stirring. The reaction was initiated by addition of 14 g of monochloroacetic acid, and the pulp slurry was stirred for 90 min at 700 rpm. After the reaction, the pulp slurry was solvent-exchanged using distilled water at pH 7.0 and passed through a grinder (Super Masscolloider, Masuko Sangyo co., Ltd., Japan) seven times to produce the CM-CNF suspension. The operation speed was 1500 rpm, and the gap distance of grinder stones was ∼ 100 μm (Fig. S2). The final concentration of the CM-CNF suspension was about 2%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs from CM-CNF suspensions
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs were synthesized via a one-pot solvothermal method employing a mass ratio of carboxymethylated-cellulose nanofiber (CM-CNF) to ethylene diamine (EDA) of 4:1. Briefly, CM-CNF (0.5 g in 25 mL of distilled water) and EDA (5 mL) were blended and stirred for 30 min. Later, the suspension was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 6 h for in-situ synthesis of GQDs from CM-CNF. The formation of GQDs from CM-CNFs was optimized with varying concentration of EDA (0.25, 0.5, 0.75, and 1), reaction time (2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 h), solvent (ethanol, methanol, propanol, and water), and pH of water (2, 4, 7, 9, and 11). The obtained GQD solution was filtered using a Nylon 66 membrane (58060-U, Supelco, 0.2 μm pores) to remove the residual CM-CNFs. The filtered solution was purified using a dialysis membrane of 2KDa for 12 h, such that the GQDs remained in the DI water.

Analytical characterizations were carried out using UV–visible spectra obtained from Varian Cary 50 and a dual beam micro-volume UV–visible spectrophotometer (SpectraLab Scientific Inc., Markham, Canada) consisting of a Czerny-Turner monochromator with a xenon pulse lamp and dual Si diode detectors. The luminescence images of the obtained GQDs irradiated at 365 nm were produced using an ATTO UV transilluminator. Photoluminescent (PL) spectra were chronicled using a SCINO FS-2 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Scinco Co. Ltd. Gangnam-gu, Seoul, Korea) equipped with FluoroMaster Plus software and a 150 W continuous-wave xenon arc lamp with a high-performance R-928 photomultiplier tube detector. The x-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were acquired using a high-resolution x-ray diffractometer (Rigaku Rint 2200 series, Rigaku Corporation, Tokyo, Japan Cu Kα radiation, 3 KW) at a wavelength of 1.5406 Å with a motorized slit and 2-bounce monochromator. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Nicolet iS5, Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) was utilized to determine the chemical structure (scan number 64, resolution 4 cm−1, wavelength 4000–400 cm−1). Raman spectral analyses were obtained using a micro-Raman system (DongWoo 500i, Dongwoo Optron, Gwangju-si, Korea) Ramboss star microscope with a He-Ne laser beam at 633 nm.

Morphological characterizations were carried out using a JEM-F200 field emission transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM, Thermal FEG Type, JEOL Ltd, Japan) with an HT of 200 kV and lattice resolution of 0.10 nm and EDS tomography (JEOL Dual SDD type). To obtain TEM images of CM-CNFs, 0.05 wt% of a CM-CNF solution was placed on a carbon copper grid and was negatively stained with 10 μL uranyl acetate solution (1% w/v). The width of the CM-CNFs was measured using ImageJ (1.52a NIH, Bethesda, MD, USA). The sizes of GQDs were measured using a Gatan microscopy suite consisting of a Digital Micrograph (Gatan Inc., Pleasanton, CA, USA). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was conducted using a JPK Nanowizard II (JPK Instruments, Bruker, Germany) bioatomic force microscope with the sample placed on a slide (measured in contact mode).

Surface chemistry was characterized using ESCA equipment (Axis SupraTM, Kratos, UK) consisting of an x-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) and ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS). XPS and UPS were utilized for analyzing electronic binding energy, chemical bonding state, relative quantification, and work function. The samples were prepared by spin coating in silicon wafer slides forming a thin film ≤ 1 μm. The obtained XPS spectrum was calibrated based on the C1s peak by correcting the C–C peak position to 284.4 eV. Molecular structures of the synthesized GQDs were determined (1H and 13C) by observing the resonance state of the nucleus in a magnetic field at 600 MHz using a high-resolution nuclear magnetic resonance spectrometer (AVANCE 600, Bruker, Germany). The obtained NMR spectrum was assessed using Jeol DeltaTM NMR data processing software for data presentation and plotting.

A stock solution of cTnI biomarker (2 μM) was prepared in PBS medium (0.01 M, pH 7.4) and was stored at 0 °C. Desired analyte concentrations were freshly diluted in PBS from stock solutions and were utilized for sensor studies. Furthermore, 5 μL of immunogen cTnI biomarker was drop-cast on the sensor platform and was kept intact at 4 °C for 1 h. After incubation, developed ITO electrodes were assessed for electrochemical sensing evaluations using voltammetric, chronoamperometric, and impedimetric studies. All the electrode and electrochemical measurements were performed in quintuplicate.

The synthesized QDs were conjugated with an EDC-NHS complex via a coupling method. The detailed procedures were as follows: 5 mL of GQDs were dispersed in 5 mL of PBS buffer blended with EDC (5 mg/mL, 100 μL) and NHS (5 mg/mL, 100 μL) for activating the surface to immobilize the antibodies with vigorous stirring for 45 min, and the unbound EDC-NHS complex was recovered by centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 10 min. Certain quantities of detection antibodies were added to the GQD solution by vigorous shaking for 1 h. Later, a BSA solution was added to block the lingering functional groups. For fluorescent sensor studies, the concentration of cTnI biomarker was varied and added to vials containing mAb cTnI conjugated with GQDs for 1 h gestation at 4 °C. To observe the PL intensity, samples were transferred to a microplate reader measuring intensity at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm and emission wavelength at 450 nm. All experimental evaluations were assessed in quintuplicate.

Electrochemical measurements were carried out using a conventional three-electrode configuration. The Ag/AgCl electrode was used as a reference, a Pt wire was used as a counter electrode, and a flexible ITO film served as a working electrode. The flexible ITO electrode was sliced into a 1 × 1 cm2 area as the working area. After preliminary rinsing with PBS, the ITO surface was modified with 5 μL of an aqueous dispersion of GQDs/C (GQDs conjugated EDC-NHS, 2 mg/mL) and was incubated at room temperature for 30 min to achieve a uniform layer on the working electrode surface. Immobilization of monoclonal anti-cardiac troponin I (cTnI mAb) was conducted by drop-casting 5 μL (50 μM) of antibodies on the surface of the bio-affinity layer of the EDC/NHS complex coupled to QDs, followed by storage at 4 °C for 1 h. The unbound antibodies were washed from the electrode surface with PBS buffer (5 s), followed by application of BSA solution (2 μL, 0.5% w/v) as a blocker to avoid non-specific interactions. Later, upon treating with PBS, the as-prepared QDs/C-based cTnI mAb immunosensor platform was assessed to probe cTnI biomarkers.

Herein, we report a facile and simple approach of synthesizing GQDs from CM-CNFs rich in cellulose and hemicellulose glucose polysaccharides that are a thick gel carbon source that thins upon agitation. The less viscous CM-CNFs were blended with EDA (a reducing agent) and were stirred for 30 min to obtain a homogeneous suspension. They were later transferred into an autoclave for reaction at 160 °C for 6 h. EDA readily reacted with CM-CNFs since they possess functional moieties of hydroxyl (–OH), carboxyl (–COOH), and carbonyl (-C = O) groups on the surface [24]. After the reaction, the solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm nylon membrane to remove the CM-CNF residues, followed by dialysis at 2KDa for 24 h to remove impurities (Scheme 1 ). Emission of bluish fluorescence from the final mixture under UV illumination confirmed the formation of GQDs that could be used for fluorescent and electrochemical detection of cTnI.

The absorption spectrum of GQDs exhibited sharp absorption peaks at 246, 282, and 345 nm due to the π-π* (polyaromatic C = C) bonds in the graphitic core and the n-π* electronic transitions of C = O/C-N bonds. The sharp absorption peak at 246 nm was ascribed to the existence of sp3-sp2 carbon lattices, signifying that synthesized GQDs were homogeneously distributed in solution (Fig. 1 a). The fluorescence spectrum of GQDs was examined for fixed excitation (390 nm, FWHM is 103.1 nm) and emission wavelengths (445 nm, FWHM was 78.3 nm), and the synthesized GQDs appeared as transparent liquids in normal light (Fig. 1b inset A) and showed bluish luminescence upon UV irradiation at 365 nm (Fig. 1b inset B). These changes promoted structural deviations mediated by chemical hybridization between graphitic networks. Furthermore, the PL spectrum was assessed to define the excitation-dependent emission spectrum, the maximum of which was observed at 445 nm; GQDs showed maximum excitation at 390 nm (Fig. 1c). Two noticeable PL peaks were attributed to the oxygenated functional groups (–COOH) and aromatic (C–C) linkages associated with the electrostatic and π-π interactions at the edge/basal planes, respectively. In this work, we further optimized the synthesis procedure at various time intervals, pH values, and for various solvents. A significant bathochromic shift was detected in emissive wavelengths due to the dielectric constant of the solvents. Due to protonation of ions, synthesized GQDs displayed green, pink, and blue luminescence (Figure S1). These phenomena were attributed to an increasing dielectric constant due to differences in the polarizability of the solvents. Such variations in solvent, pH, and time-dependent observations helped us identify the optimal GQDs for sensor studies.

The formation of crystalline GQDs was confirmed by XRD analysis. A prominent peak at the 20.4° (002) lattices indicated the orientation of the graphitic surface (Fig. 1d). FTIR spectral analysis was conducted to detect the functionalization of moieties on the graphitic surface and is depicted in Fig. 1e. The synthesized GQDs displayed a broad peak at 3317 cm−1, and the small peak around 2966 cm−1 corresponded to –OH vibrations, while the peak at 1702 cm−1 was attributed to C = O stretching. The peak in the fingerprint region at 1625 cm−1 indicates N–H bending, while that at 1539 cm−1 is related to N-O stretching (nitro compounds with strong interactions). The peak at 1457 cm−1 was attributed to the C-O–H hydrocarbons and amine groups of GQDs, that at 1089 cm−1 was associated with amine alkyl groups, 884 cm−1 belonged to C = C stretching, and 812 cm−1 corresponded to C–H bending [25]. Raman spectra were obtained to probe the structural and vibrational modes of GQDs exhibiting peaks at 1336 and 1470 cm−1, related to the respective D and G bands of GQDs (Fig. 1f). The intensity ratio (ID/IG) was estimated to be 0.9813, which indicated higher defects and heterogeneous moieties on graphitic carbon systems that can support electron-hole regeneration appropriate for electronic transition coupling to the surface of GQDs. The peak at 1470 cm−1 was attributed to the C-O–H of the hydrogen bonds and amine groups of GQDs.

The TEM images of as-synthesized CM-CNFs are visualized in Figures S2a and b, exhibiting a fiber diameter ≤ 50 nm. Fig. 2 a-d show TEM images of the GQDs derived from CM-CNFs synthesized at a temperature of 160 °C. These images show relatively small dots homogeneously distributed in the range of 1–5 nm (primarily at 4 nm), as indicated in Fig. 2k. The high-resolution (HRTEM) images (Fig. 2e and f) signified high crystallinity of GQDs, with a lattice parameter of 0.330 nm (002) like), which is similar to that of the lattice fringes of the graphitic network. The edge structures of the GQDs from HRTEM images were in zigzag and armchair edge modules of graphitic transitions based on measurements probing the GQD features (Figure S2c) [26]. Graphitic networks tended to possess zigzag edges with specific electronic and magnetic transitions, and the edge structures of GQDs are displayed in HRTEM images. Fig. 2g shows a 2D FFT image and schematic model of zigzag and armchair modules principally positioned in parallel orientations of the hexagonal graphitic network. The GQD domain structure resembled the sp2 carbon lattices in carbonized fiber pitches representing cleaved off and unzipped planar graphitic domains associated with the surface functionalities used to probe the zigzag alignment [27]. The AFM images (Fig. 2h-j) supported topographic morphologies of GQD heights of 1 to 4 nm, relating to 1–3 graphene layers.

Zeta potential analysis was used to assess the surface charges and sizes of the GQDs along with modification of EDC/NHS complexes on GQDs, on which immobilized anti-cTnI and cTnI antigens changed the surface charges due to conjugation. GQDs were negatively charged because of the constituent carboxyl, carbonyl, and hydroxyl moieties on the backbone graphitic structure (Fig. 2i). The zeta-potential value of GQDs displayed a negative value of −25 mV, confirming a negatively charged surface with a size of ∼ 4 nm. After functionalization of QDs with an EDC/NHS complex, their surface charges were enhanced and estimated to be + 42 mV, suggesting a stable solution and a positive surface. After immobilizing the anti-cTnI on the conjugated GQDs, the surface charge was altered to −14 mV, exhibiting a stable conjugation possessing a negative charge (Figure S3).

NMR spectral analysis was conducted to identify the structural modifications of the CM-CNFs after conjugation with EDA, and a plausible synthesis mechanism for formation of GQDs is illustrated in Figure S4. Fig. 3 a shows the proton (1H) resonance spectrum along with magnified regions used to evaluate the functional moieties along with their respective chemical shift (ppm) and peak multitudes. We were able to observe singlet, doublet of doublet, triplet, septet, and quadruplet peaks corresponding to the chemical shift of 0.88 for alkanes; 1.17 – 1.87 for alcohols, aldehydes, and ketones; 2–3 for cycloalkanes, diallyl amides, and pyrrolidine; 3.6 – 4.4 for ethers and nitro-alkanes; 7.14 for imidazole; 7.57 for benzaldehydes; and 8.52 for pyridine moieties [28–30]. Proton shifts were correlated with allyl acetylation, ascribed to amidic rings derived from EDA (Fig. 3c-h). The conversion mechanism of polysaccharides to graphitic surfaces was confirmed by 13C NMR, elucidating the changes in carbon skeletons (Fig. 3b). Carbon moieties on the surface were interchanged into diallyl acetamide, alcohol, and diallyl carbonates with a sharp peak (Fig. 3i-k). Furthermore, simulated 1H and 13C NMR spectra obtained by prediction using ChemNMR 1H and 13C of ChemBioDraw Ultra 14.0 were similar to experimentally obtained spectra, marking the cleavage of cellulose polysaccharides to GQD formation and changes in orientation of the carbon skeleton at respective sites.

To complement 1H and 13C NMR and FTIR spectral analyses, the surface chemistry and functional moieties of GQDs conjugated with biomarker proteins were further assessed by XPS and UPS. Fig. 4 a depicts the full survey spectrum of GQDs before and after sensing the cTnI biomarker protein. The deconvoluted C1s spectra (Fig. 4b) of GQDs and GQDs/C + Ab-Ag exhibiting C–C moieties were associated with a binding energy of 284.4 eV. GQDs tended to possess sp3 graphitic hybridization with a deconvoluted peak at 285.1 eV along with C–H at 287.7 eV [32]. Upon interaction of biomarker proteins, functional moieties were modified to possess C-N and OC = O at binding energies of 286.2 eV and 288.3 eV, respectively. In the N1s deconvoluted spectrum of pristine GQDs, C = N-C and C-NH2 were detected at respective binding energies of 399.2 eV and 397.1 eV. These peaks shifted due to interactions with cTnI biomarkers to 399.1 eV and 400.6 eV respectively related to C = N-C and N-(C = O)-O moieties, proving the sensor response. [31,33] Upon interactions with biomarkers, O1s spectra exhibited the characteristic bonds of C-O, OC = O, and C-OH at binding energies of 529.7, 530.6 and 531.4 eV, respectively. Pristine GQDs showed deconvoluted O1s peaks at 529.1 and 530.5 eV, which belonged to functional moieties of C-O and OC = O bonds, indicating effective interactions between amine and carboxyl groups of pristine GQDs with cTnI biomarkers, in agreement with NMR and FTIR spectra. For conjugated GQDs/C (EDC/NHS complex), high-resolution C1s peaks were observed at 284.65 eV and 286.20 eV belonging to respective C–C and C-N. The N1s peaks at a binding energy of 399.12 eV representing C = N-C and of deconvoluted O1s peaks at 529.03 eV and 530.67 eV corresponded to respective C-O and OC = O, signifying that the surfaces of the quantum dots primarily contained carbonyls, carboxyls, and amide bonds (Figure S5) [34]. Upon immobilizing the conjugated quantum dots with cTnI antibodies, the surface properties were altered to C–C bonds, sp2 graphitic hybridizations, and C–H bonds at 284.49 eV, 285.93 eV and 287.24 eV, respectively. N1s deconvoluted spectra at 399.4 eV and 401.13 eV corresponded to respective C = N-C and N-(C = O)-O. The O1s spectra at 528.92 eV, 530.2 eV, and 532.37 eV were produced by respective C-O, OC = O, and C-OH peaks, suggesting that the edge/planar lattices of carbon networks became more hydrophilic (Figure S6). The atomic and mass concentrations of the elements presented in samples are depicted in Figure S7.

UPS analysis was conducted to explain the functional changes by measuring the work function and ionization energy, which were expected to change with electron density of basal planar/edge lattices [35]. The work function and ionization energy were estimated to be 4.3 eV (pristine GQDs) or 4.8 eV (GQDs/C + Ab-Ag) and 5.7 eV relative to the interactions between the amine and oxygen functional moieties leading to activation of disorderly arrangements at π-π transitions. The charge transfer and dipole moments between the pristine GQDs and those after conjugation with cTnI increased, which confirmed the change in energy resulting in expulsion of the electronic interfacial area [36]. The increase in work function signified the base changes due to interactions on the graphitic surfaces of GQDs. This was attributed to the electronegativity of the nitrogen moiety, which promoted electron donation/acceptance with a material suitable as an immunosensor [37].

The optical functionalities of GQDs were observed in PL emissive studies via a fluorescence quenching immunoassay strategy (Figures S8a and b). Initially, an aqueous nano-dispersion of GQDs was subjected to a fluorescence quenching immunoassay of cTnI biomarkers with colloidal targeting of IgG mAb to provide a unified moiety of EDC/NHS conjugation. A bio-interfacial layer with high concentrations of 1° amine and hydroxyl (1° at C-6 and 2° at C-3) promoted the reactive moiety at the GQD surface. GQD surface structures were enriched by conjugation of multiple binding sites, enhancing the synergistic localization of heavy chains of cTnI mAb. The energy transfer from excited GQDs was compared to the antibody conjugation by modifying the GQD surfaces by EDC/NHS complex tuning [38]. This allowed tuning of the electronic transitions of the semiconductor by quenching the fluorescence activity upon immobilization. Figure S8c depicts the fluorescence quenching behavior of GQDs/C + Ab before and after binding with varying concentrations of cTnI, resulting in immunocomplex formation of cTnI biomarker proteins (500 – 0.5 nM). Figure S8d displays a histogram of the relative fluorescence, suggesting that the immunocomplex with cTnI biomarkers exhibits linear quenching behavior. We employed the empirical expression ΔF = (F0 – Ft) / F0 to model this behavior, where F0 is the fluorescence intensity in the absence of cTnI biomarker, and Ft is the fluorescence intensity in the presence of cTnI proteins. The regression equation for the developed system was y = -0.0971 × + 0.5881, with R2 = 0.9871 and LOD = 30 pM (Figure S8e). From the obtained results, we predicted that GQDs synergized surface moieties by immobilizing a bio-interfacial layer suitable for immunodetection of the cTnI biomarker, suitable for use in healthcare diagnostics.

Electrochemical immunosensor platforms for synthesized GQDs were assessed by targeting the acute myocardial infection-linked biomarker and cTnI protein sensor fabrication as displayed in Fig. 5 . Initially, GQDs were modified on flexible ITO substrate for developing the immunosensor. This allowed scalable applications and disposable sensors. Novel electrode materials are important when using a flexible sensor matrix, and retention of receptor bioactivity with the target marker or protein was challenging. The structural affinity of the fabricated sensor material was tuned for optimal immunoassay performance by modifying the surface with EDC/NHS complex to form a bio-interfacial layer for binding the cTnI mAb (IgG isotype) [39]. The sensor substrate mAb bonds to the epitope of the constant fragment (Fc), permitting the paratope of the antigen-binding fragment (Fab) to bind to the target biomarker. Such interactions increase the resistance of the electrodes, significantly quenching the electron transfer across the interfacial layer and boosting immunosensor performance. A CV study was carried out with various concentrations of cTnI Ab to optimize the Ab concentration based on the obtained current (Figure S9).

For cTnI sensor assembly, basic electrochemical characterization was assessed using CV and EIS studies (Fig. 6 a and b). Electrochemical characterization studies were performed using a redox probe, K[Fe(CN)6]3-/4-, as synthesized GQDs, conjugated GQDs, and immobilized mAb produced non-faradaic current responses. CVs observed for the bare ITO electrode exhibited redox peak potentials at 0.152 V and 0.078 V with a variation (ΔEp) of 74 mV at an approximate peak current (Ip) of 4 μA (substantial for a redox probe.). Drop-casting GQDs on the ITO electrode surface increased the current (Ip of) to 10 μA with shifts in peak potentials at 0.188 V and 0.036 V, indicating that GQDs were conductive in nature. Pristine GQDs tend to follow EC mechanism i.e., reversible electron transfer followed by irreversible homogeneous chemical reaction. Such a type of reaction usually occurs while moving the system from equilibrium to non-Nernstian response. The shift in peak potential is attributed to the intramolecular decomposition of reduction/oxidation of surface moieties. [40] The current decreased after depositing GQDs/C because the EDC/NHS complex conjugated with GQDs, and the peak potential shifted to 0.308 V and 0.064 V, signifying the semiconductive nature of the electrode surface. Immobilizing the mAb of cTnI on the surface of conjugated GQDs further decreased the current and increased the peak potential, hindering electron transfer. This explains the resistance developed on the interfacial layers. Faradaic Nyquist plot analysis also validated the CV results. The impedimetric data revealed the energy barrier formed on the electrode surface probed through the redox mediators. The obtained charge-transfer resistance (Rct) was calculated by extrapolating the EIS curve to Randle’s equivalent circuit diagram, providing additional information about the double-layer capacitance (Cdl) and Warburg resistance (W) through redox-mediated probes. The acquired values are presented in Table S1. Hence, both CV and EIS analyses confirmed the development of steric hindrance on the bio-interfacial layer of the fabricated electrode surface.

The effect of scan rate (20 – 200 mV/s) on the GQDs/ITO surface is depicted in Fig. 6c, showing a linear increase in current. This demonstrated a surface-constrained process at the electrode/electrolyte interface. From the scan-rate studies, we confirmed that the electrochemical reaction at the interface of the electrode was a surface-confined process (Fig. 6d). The electrochemical reversibility of the modified electrode was verified by the Fe(CN)6 3-/4-, through which electrochemical surface area was estimated using the Randles-Sevcik expression:
(1)
I = 2.69 ×
10 5
n
3 / 2
A
D
1 / 2
C
υ
1 / 2
Here, I is the peak current (μA), n is the number of electrons engaged in the redox probes, A is the electroactive surface area (cm2), D is the diffusion coefficient (6.5 × 10-6 cm2/s), C is the concentration of the redox probe, and υ is the scan rate. The electrochemical active surface area of the modified electrode GQDs was 0.36 cm2, GQDs/C was 0.44 cm2, GQDs/C + Ab was 0.58 cm2, and GQDs/C + Ab-Ag was 0.66 cm2 (Fig. 6e). The thickness of the diffusion layer GQDs/C + Ab-Ag@ITO was estimated using
(2)
δ =
π D t
1 / 2
where δ is the thickness of the diffusion layer (cm), D is the diffusion coefficient (6.5 × 10-6 cm2/s), and t is the time (s). The thickness of the modified electrode GQDs/C + Ab-Ag was estimated to be 350 μm, which was evident at the interfacial electrode layer (Fig. 6f).

The modest immune complex configuration of the interfacial electrode was confirmed using the linear sweep and differential pulse voltametric (LSV, DPV) techniques. Specifically, we assessed the cTnI biomarker protein concentration-dependent variation in the anodic peak responses of GQDs/C + Ab-modified electrodes (Fig. 7 a, c). The anodic peak current of GQDs/C + Ab was high (about 41 μA), while the peak current was quenched to 36.5 μA for a concentration of 500 nM upon conjugating with cTnI protein. This reverse chronological increase of electrochemical resistance was proportional to cTnI concentration and promoted effective immune complex formation at the interface, which was affirmed through both LSV and DPV techniques. The variations in current responses against cTnI protein were observed in linear fits in Fig. 7b and d, suggesting that localization of electronic transitions tuned the GQDs into electro-active probes, altering the oxygen and nitrogen moieties on the edge/planar lattices. The linear regression model for LSV analysis was estimated to be y = 5.039 × + 23.375, with R2 = 0.9901, and that for the DPV analysis was y = 11.718 × + 60.063, with R2 = 0.992 Additionally, the presence of pyridinic amine ligands restrained a π-π interaction within sp3 graphitized carbon matrices, representing a potent redox candidate for immunosensor studies [41]. Chronoamperometric analysis was performed to assess the appropriateness of the fabricated sensor platform for real-time utility and to complement the voltametric studies depicted in Fig. 7e. The biased potential of 0.4 V was used for the oxidation reaction of the GQDs/C + Ab-Ag-modified electrode when measuring the current against response time for various concentrations of cTnI biomarker protein. Like the voltametric (LSV and DPV) studies, the CA technique also demonstrated concentration-dependent quenching, as depicted in Fig. 7f, and showed a linear regression coefficient of 0.9942. The LOD and sensitivity of the modified electrode were estimated to be 29 pM and 15.8 μA/ nM, respectively.

Fig. 8 a depicts the impedimetric analysis to assess the interfacial electron transfer and kinetic parameters with varying concentration of cTnI biomarker proteins on the modified ITO electrode surface. Non-faradic impedimetric measurements were carried out in the frequency range of 100 mHz – 100 kHz at a potential polarization of open-circuit potential (OCP) combining electrostatic repulsion and steric hindrance [42]. Nyquist measurements were simulated using an equivalent circuit-fitting procedure with modified Randle’s equivalent circuits as exhibited in the inset of Fig. 8a. The interfacial polarization resistance increased as the concentration of cTnI biomarker increased, hindering the effect on the surface [43,44]. The Bode analysis showed that the Warburg resistance had a phase change of 30° and frequency peak at 2 Hz (Fig. 8b)), corroborating the increase in resistance for varying concentrations of cTnI. A linear relationship between resistance polarization and cTnI biomarker concentration ranged from 0.5 – 500 nM, estimating a slope of 99.69 and a linear regression of 0.9968 (Fig. 8c). Table S2 shows that the Rct value changed systematically at each modification step. These results confirmed electrode modification at each step, in agreement with CV results discussed earlier.

The repetitive nature of the developed sensor was assessed with five electrodes at a specific concentration of 100 nM cTnI target analyte, all of which and produced a similar current response, with an RSD of 1.4%, as displayed in Figures S10a and b. This proves the robustness of the fabricated sensor. Additionally, specificity of the fabricated GQDs/C + Ab complex was utilized against interferents (Bilirubin, BSA, C-RP and IgG) as shown in the voltametric histogram in Figure S10c with respect to cTnI (100 nM) and interferents (500 nM)). This demonstrates the ability of the established system to discern molecules. Pristine interferents (Bilirubin, BSA, C-RP, and IgG) were not major contaminants because the paratopes of the immobilized mAb displayed a low current compared to the cTnI target specificity. Based on the studied outcomes, GQDs obtained from CM-CNFs allowed conjugation of the bio-interfacial layer and immune receptors suitable for tag-free sensing of cardio-biomarker proteins.

In summary, biofriendly graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were developed using a facile hydrothermal method. Carboxymethylated cellulose nanofibers (CM-CNFs) acted as a radical initiator, providing reactive sites at the planar lattices. Spectroscopic analysis revealed the carboxyl, carbonyl, and amine moieties in CNFs in conjugation of graphitic moieties. An aqueous colloidal dispersion of GQDs exhibited fluorescence emission at 445 nm because of the intramolecular charge transfer, which could also be utilized for bioimaging. Immobilizing the interfacial layer facilitated the mAb of cTnI conjugates on the surface by EDC/NHS complex, indicating the influence of specific cTnI candidates. As shown, the twin functional immunoprobe was easy to assemble and showed high sensitivity feasible for cTnI protein sensing with a rapid analytical response time (<1 min). Further optimization of the purported immunosensor design must be improved in multiplexed analysis to discern the serotypes of cardiac proteins.
Data will be made available on request.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
